text: The video demonstrates the extrahepatic approach for a right posterior sectionectomy. The tumor is located in the posterior section of the liver, which is deep within the rib cage. Four 8mm trocars are positioned in the upper quadrant of the abdomen for robotic instruments, with trochar number four placed below the left rib cage to prevent collision with trochar number three. An assistant operates a laparoscopic instrument or suction device through a 12mm trocar.

The surgeon uses a long plastic tube to apply the Pringles maneuver through a two-millimeter incision. A cholecystectomy is performed to expose the liver hilum. Gauze is placed in the subhepatic area for cushioning and clearing. The right posterior glissonian pedicle is identified and separated from the liver parenchyma, with the rubrial groove widened for better access.

The surgeon performs dissection around the glistening pedicle at the liver hilum. The caudate branch of the polar vein is coagulated using a bipolar energy device. Dissection behind the pedicle is challenging, with small branches being carefully divided. Breathing control is maintained using bipolar forceps.

The surgeon performs pharyngeal dissection beyond the lumbar trachea, widening the space to facilitate isolation of the glistening pedicle. A string is inserted behind the pedicle and maneuvered to the opposite side.

The surgeon encircles and ligates the pad curve using a string and plastic clip. Under the PolarSense camera, the anatomical resection line is established for the right posterior sectionectomy. Parenchymal dissection is performed along this line using crushing and bipolar coagulation, with fine dissection aided by the active blade vibration of an energy device. The pathway of the right posterior glycemic particle is identified, and small brain T is resected.

The surgeon divides the previously clipped right posterior glycemic particle and applies the Pringles maneuver to minimize bleeding and clear the operative field. Using bipolar crushing and blade fine dissection, the intersectional pharyngeal dissection progresses. During deep camera dissection, the right hepatic vein is encountered, and a small branch is isolated and divided. The caudate lobe is transected, exposing the inferior vena cava. A large right inferior hepatic vein is resected after securing it with plastic clips.

The surgeon completes the liver parenchymal dissection around the right hepatic vein. The specimen is detached from the diaphragm through dissection in the bare area and placed into a bin bag. Abdominal pressure is applied while irrigation and hemostasis are performed.
